content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660                322-5901              322-6441              322-6444

March 23, 1983


Ellyn S. Levinson
Deputy Attorney General
Department of Justice
3580 Wilshire Blvd., Rm. 800
Los Angeles, CA  90010

Re:  Opinion Request No. 83-001
     Our Advice No. A-83-073

Dear Ms. Levinson:

     Thank you for your opinion request received by this office
on March 9, 1983.  Because your question does not raise a
substantial question of interpretation under the Political
Reform Act, no formal opinion will be issued in response to it.
Instead, we are answering your question by this letter, which
constitutes advice under Government Code Section 83114.1/  If,
after receiving this letter, you still want a Commission
opinion, you may request the Commission to review our denial of
your request for a formal opinion pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18321.

FACTS

     You have asked whether the Santa Monica Mountains
Conservancy is exempt from Section 84308, and, if it is not
exempt, what specific types of actions by the Conservancy come
within the coverage of Section 84308.  In particular, you asked
whether any of the following types of actions are covered by
Section 84308:

     1.  Acceptance of offers of dedication of open-space;

     2.  Grants to other public agencies (and possibly also to
non-profit corporations) for acquisition of property and
development of trails;

     3.  Leases of buildings on state lands to caretakers and
docent groups; and

_____________________
1/  All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise stated.

Ellyn S. Levinson
March 23, 1983
Page 2


     4. License agreements with adjacent landowners and tenants
authorizing use of state property for certain purposes.

                         CONCLUSION

     The Santa Monica Mountains Conservancy is not exempt from
Section 84308. However, it is not covered by Section 84308 when
it engages in activities (1) and (2), since these do not involve
licenses, permits or other entitlements for use. On the other
hand, it is covered when it engages in activities (3) and (4),
which do involve licenses, permits or other entitlements for
use.

                         ANALYSIS

     Section 84308(d) exempts only "legislative bodies such as
city councils, county boards of supervisors, and the State
Legislature ... from the coverage of this section." The
Commission, by regulations, has defined what types of bodies are
exempted under Section 84308(d). 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18348.1(a) provides that:

          A "legislative body" while acting as a body or
     committee thereof is excluded by Government Code
     Section 84308(d) from the coverage of Government Code
     Sections 84308(a), (b) and (c) if its entire membership
     consists of elected officers directly elected by the
     voters to serve on that body. As used therein, the term
     "voters" has the same meaning as in Elections Code
     Section 18.

     Since the members of the Santa Monica Mountains Conservancy are
not directly elected by the voters, (Public Resources Code,
Section 33200) the Conservancy is not exempt from the coverage
of Section 84308 as a "legislative body." Members or alternates
of an agency which is not exempt under Section 84308(d) are
covered by Section 84308(a) through (c) whenever the agency acts
on licenses, permits, and "other entitlements for use."

     The Commission has defined the term "license, permit or
other entitlement for use" in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18438.2(a). Applying this regulation to the activities of the
Conservancy described in your letter, it is our view that at
least two of these activities come within the coverage of
Section 84308. Specifically, you asked about the following four
activities:

Ellyn S. Levinson
March 23, 1983
Page 3


1. Acceptance of offers of dedication of open space
pursuant to Public Resources Code Section 33207. This activity
does not involve a license, permit, or other entitlement for
use, and is not covered by Section 84308.

2. Grants to other public agencies and non-profit corpora-
tions for acquisition of property or development of trails
pursuant to Public Resources Code Section 33204. Assuming that
a grant from the Conservancy is not a prerequisite to the
acquisition of property or development of trails (i.e., the
other agency or non-profit corporation, if it had the money,
would be free to do these things without the Conservancy's
permission or consent), these activities would not be covered by
Section 84308.

3. Lease of buildings on state property to caretakers and
docent groups pursuant to Public Resources Code Sections 33203,
33206. The lease activity appears to come within 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18438.2(a)(2). Permitting docent groups to conduct
tours may also constitute a license to carry on business
activity, which comes within Section 18438.2(a)(3). Therefore
these activities are covered by Section 84308.

4. Licenses for use of state property for certain purposes
pursuant to Public Resources Code Sections 33203 and 33206. As
a license activity, this is within the meaning of 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18438.2(a)(2). Proceedings relating to licenses
allowing for use of public lands are covered by Section 84308.

You also asked, in a telephone conversation, whether the
advisory board to the Conservancy is covered by Section 84308.
If the board is "purely advisory," such that it does not need a
conflict of interest code, it would not be covered by Section
84308. It is my understanding that this is the case. If it is
not purely advisory, however, some of its activities may be
covered by Section 84308.

I hope that this answers your questions. If you have a
question as to any particular decision or action, our Legal
Division will be happy to provide more specific advice.

Very truly yours,

Ted Prim
Executive Director

BAM:km

JOHN K. VAN DE KAMP
Attorney General

State of California
DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE

EPPC

3580 WILSHIRE BOULEVARD, ROOM 800
LOS ANGELES 90010
(213) 736-2304

MAR 9 1258 PM '83

March 3, 1983

Ted Prin
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" St.
Sacramento, CA 95804

Request No.     83-001

Date Received  March 9, 1983

Response Due  March 23, 1983

Dear Mr. Prin:

This office represents the Santa Monica Mountains
Conservancy. That agency has sought our advice concerning
the applicability of the new campaign contribution law (AB
1040, Chap. 1049, Statutes 1982.) to its members. While we
are able to give the Conservancy our views, it is your
opinion which is binding. Therefore, on behalf of the
Conservancy, we request that you issue an opinion pursuant
to Government Code § 83115(a) determining whether the
provisions of AB 1040 apply to the Conservancy members.

It may assist you to know that the Santa Monica Mountains
Conservancy Act is codified in sections 33200 et seq. of the
Public Resources Code. If you determine that that statutory
scheme authorizes the Conservancy to exercise quasi-judicial
powers, please define what types of activities constitute
"other entitlements for use". The Conservancy does not
issue permits or licenses, but through it's acquisition
program it does decide how certain parcels of property will
be used. Title 2 California Administrative Code §
18438.2(a) as drafted, and perhaps as filed with the
Secretary of State, states that "An 'application for . . .
other entitlement for use'" includes "(2) any land use
permit, license or other action relating to zoning variances, subdivision maps, building permits, leases of
public land or facilities, or other similar proceedings."
This regulation does not make it clear which, if any, of the
actions taken by the Conservancy involve an entitlement for
use.

Specific examples of Conservancy action include: (1)
acceptance of offers of dedication of open-space (such
offers of dedication are often imposed by local jurisdictions
as a condition to approval of a subdivision tract map), (2)
grants to other public agencies for acquisition of property
and development of trails (legislation may be introduced
this year which would also authorize the Conservancy to

Ted Prim
March 3, 1983
Page 2


award grants to non-profit corporations for property
acquisition, management and maintenance, and trail
development), (3) lease of buildings located on state
property to caretakers of that property and docent groups
and (4) license agreements with adjacent landowners and
tenants authorizing use of state property for certain
purposes (such as ingress and egress to the adjacent
properties). Your opinion on whether any of these actions
constitutes issuance of an entitlement for use would be
appreciated. If you need additional factual details, please
feel free to contact me.

Sincerely,

JOHN K. VAN DE KAMP
Attorney General

ELLYN S. LEVINSON
Deputy Attorney General

ESL:ht
cc: Joseph T. Edmiston
    Executive Director
    Santa Monica Mountains Conservancy

use.

Division will be happy to provide more specific advice.

CONCLUSION: The Santa Monica Mountains Conservancy is not exempt from 
Section 84308. 
However, it is not covered by Section 84308 when 
it engages in activities (1) and (2) f since these do not involve 
licenses, permits or other entitlements for use. 
On the other 
hand, it is covered when it engages in activities (3) and (4),
